docs.title: 1. What Is CentOS?
docs.text:
CentOS (Community ENTerprise Operating System) is a free, open-source Linux distribution that is built from the same source code as Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL).

In simple terms:

  • RHEL → paid, enterprise support
  • CentOS → free, community version of RHEL

Because of this, CentOS is:

  • Very stable
  • Widely used on servers
  • Popular for learning Linux system administration

If you learn CentOS, you can easily work with RHEL and many cloud servers.

docs.title: 2.1 CentOS Linux (Traditional)
docs.text:
• Versions like CentOS 7
  • Very stable
  • Common in tutorials and older servers
  • CentOS 7 is still widely used

docs.title: 2.2 CentOS Stream (New Model)
docs.text:
• Rolling-release, sits between Fedora and RHEL
  • Receives updates earlier than RHEL
  • More “preview” style

👉 For beginners: If you see CentOS 7, it is perfectly fine to learn with it.

docs.title: 3. Where Is CentOS Used?
docs.text:
CentOS is mainly used in:

  • Web servers (Apache / Nginx)
  • Database servers (MySQL, PostgreSQL)
  • Big data (Hadoop, Spark)
  • DevOps / CI systems
  • Cloud & virtual machines

You usually do not install CentOS on laptops. It’s mostly used on servers or virtual machines.

docs.title: 4. Basic CentOS Concepts You Must Know
4.1 Linux Is Case-Sensitive
docs.text: File.txt ≠ file.txt

docs.title: 4.2 Everything Is a File
docs.text:
• Devices
  • Disks
  • Network
  • Processes

All are represented as files in Linux.

docs.title: 5. Logging into CentOS
docs.text:
Usually you connect via SSH:

ssh username@server_ip

After login, you’ll see a shell prompt like:

[user@localhost ~]$

docs.title: 6.1 Where Am I?
docs.text: pwd

docs.title: 6.2 List Files
docs.text:
ls
ls -l
ls -a

docs.title: 6.3 Change Directory
docs.text:
cd /etc
cd ~
cd ..

docs.title: 6.4 Create Files and Directories
docs.text:
touch file.txt
mkdir mydir

docs.title: 6.5 Copy, Move, Delete
docs.text:
cp a.txt b.txt
mv a.txt dir/
rm a.txt
rm -r mydir

⚠️ Be careful with rm -r — no recycle bin.

docs.title: 7.1 View Text Files
docs.text:
cat file.txt
less file.txt

docs.title: 7.2 View Logs
docs.text:
less /var/log/messages

Press:

  • q → quit
  • /text → search

docs.title: 8. Package Management (Very Important)
docs.text: CentOS uses yum (CentOS 7) or dnf (newer).

docs.title: 8.1 Install Software
docs.text: sudo yum install vim

docs.title: 8.2 Remove Software
docs.text: sudo yum remove vim

docs.title: 8.3 Update System
docs.text: sudo yum update

docs.title: 9.1 Root vs Normal User
docs.text:
• root → super administrator
  • normal user → limited permissions

Use sudo to run admin commands:

sudo systemctl status sshd

docs.title: 9.2 File Permissions
docs.text:
ls -l

Example:

-rw-r--r-- 1 user user file.txt

Meaning:

  • owner can read/write
  • group can read
  • others can read

docs.title: 10. Service Management (systemctl)
docs.text: CentOS uses systemd.

docs.title: 10.1 Check Service Status
docs.text: systemctl status sshd

docs.title: 10.2 Start / Stop / Restart
docs.text:
sudo systemctl start nginx
sudo systemctl stop nginx
sudo systemctl restart nginx

docs.title: 10.3 Enable at Boot
docs.text: sudo systemctl enable nginx

docs.title: 11.1 Check IP Address
docs.text: ip addr

docs.title: 11.2 Test Connectivity
docs.text: ping google.com

docs.title: 12.1 Check Firewall Status
docs.text: sudo systemctl status firewalld

docs.title: 12.2 Open a Port (Example: 80)
docs.text:
sudo firewall-cmd --add-port=80/tcp --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

docs.title: 13. Logs and Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Important log locations:

/var/log/messages
/var/log/secure
/var/log/boot.log

Check logs first when something goes wrong.

docs.title: 14. Common Beginner Mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting sudo
  • Using rm -rf / (never do this!)
  • Editing system files without backup
  • Confusing CentOS with Ubuntu commands (apt ≠ yum)

docs.title: 15. What to Learn Next?
docs.text:
After this tutorial, a good learning path is:

  1. Vim basics
  2. Shell scripting
  3. SSH configuration
  4. Nginx / Apache
  5. MySQL / PostgreSQL
  6. Docker & containers

docs.title: 16. Summary
docs.text:
CentOS is:

  • Stable
  • Server-oriented
  • Perfect for learning Linux administration

If you understand:

  • file operations
  • package management
  • services
  • permissions

👉 You already have solid Linux fundamentals.

docs.title: 1. What Is YUM?
docs.text:
YUM (Yellowdog Updater, Modified) is the package manager used in CentOS 7.

In simple words:

YUM helps you install, update, remove, and manage software automatically.

Instead of downloading .rpm files manually, YUM:

  • Downloads software from repositories
  • Resolves dependencies automatically
  • Keeps your system consistent and safe

docs.title: 2. What Is a Package?
docs.text:
A package is a software bundle that contains:

  • Program files
  • Libraries
  • Configuration files
  • Version information

In CentOS, packages are usually:

*.rpm

YUM works with RPM packages but you should use YUM, not rpm, in daily work.

docs.title: 3.1 Root Permission Required
docs.text:
Most YUM commands need root privileges:

sudo yum install vim

Or switch to root:

su -

docs.title: 4.1 Install a Package
docs.text:
sudo yum install vim

YUM will:

  1. Check repositories
  2. Resolve dependencies
  3. Ask for confirmation

Type y and press Enter.

# Install a new package and assume yes to all questions (also works with update, great for automated updates):
yum -y install package

docs.title: 4.2 Remove a Package
docs.text: sudo yum remove vim

docs.title: 4.3 Update All Packages
docs.text:
sudo yum update

Update a single package:

sudo yum update nginx

docs.title: 4.4 Search for a Package
docs.text: yum search nginx

docs.title: 4.5 Get Package Information
docs.text: yum info nginx

docs.title: 5.1 List Installed Packages
docs.text: yum list installed

docs.title: 5.2 List Available Packages
docs.text: yum list available

docs.title: 5.3 Check if a Package Is Installed
docs.text: yum list installed | grep nginx

docs.title: 6.1 What Is a Repository?
docs.text:
A repository (repo) is a server that stores:

  • Software packages
  • Metadata
  • Dependency information

YUM downloads software only from enabled repositories.

docs.title: 6.2 Repo Configuration Location
docs.text:
/etc/yum.repos.d/

Each file ends with:

.repo

Example:

CentOS-Base.repo

docs.title: 6.3 View Enabled Repositories
docs.text: yum repolist

docs.title: 7.1 EPEL Repository
docs.text:
EPEL (Extra Packages for Enterprise Linux) provides many useful tools.

Install EPEL:

sudo yum install epel-release

Then:

yum repolist

docs.title: 7.2 Aliyun Mirrors
docs.text:
cd /etc/yum.repos.d
# 1. Backup original
mv CentOS-Base.repo CentOS-Base.repo.backup
# 2. Download new source
wget http://mirrors.aliyun.com/repo/Centos-7.repo
# 3. Set as default
mv Centos-7.repo CentOS-Base.repo

docs.title: 8. Clean YUM Cache
docs.text: Sometimes YUM has problems due to cache.

docs.title: 8.1 Clean Cache
docs.text: sudo yum clean all

docs.title: 8.2 Rebuild Cache
docs.text: sudo yum makecache

docs.title: 9. Install from a Local RPM File
docs.text:
If you already have an .rpm file:

sudo yum install ./package.rpm

YUM will still handle dependencies (better than rpm -ivh).

docs.title: 10.1 View History
docs.text: yum history

docs.title: 10.2 Undo a Transaction
docs.text:
sudo yum history undo 10

This can undo an install or update safely.

docs.title: 11.1 List Groups
docs.text: yum group list

docs.title: 11.2 Install a Group
docs.text: sudo yum groupinstall "Development Tools"

docs.title: 12.1 “Cannot find a valid baseurl”
docs.text:
• Network issue
  • DNS problem
  • Repo misconfiguration

Check:

ping google.com

docs.title: 12.2 Locked YUM Process
docs.text:
ps aux | grep yum

Then wait or kill the process.

docs.title: 13. Best Practices for Beginners
docs.text: ✅ Use yum instead of rpm ✅ Update system regularly ✅ Install EPEL early ✅ Read confirmation messages carefully ❌ Don’t disable core repositories blindly

docs.title: 14. YUM vs DNF (Quick Note)
docs.text:
Feature YUM DNF Used in CentOS 7 CentOS 8+ Dependency handling Good Better Speed Slower Faster

If you know YUM, learning DNF is very easy.

docs.title: 15. Summary
docs.text:
YUM helps you:

  • Install software
  • Remove software
  • Update your system
  • Manage dependencies
  • Work safely and efficiently

Essential commands to remember:

yum install
yum remove
yum update
yum search
yum info
yum repolist

docs.title: 1. What is rpm?
docs.text:
rpm stands for RPM Package Manager.

It is a low-level package management tool used mainly on:

  • RHEL
  • CentOS
  • Rocky Linux
  • AlmaLinux
  • Fedora

rpm is used to:

  • Install .rpm software packages
  • Query package information
  • Verify installed packages
  • Remove packages

📌 rpm works directly with local .rpm files It does NOT automatically resolve dependencies.

docs.title: 2. What Is an RPM Package?
docs.text:
An RPM package is a file like:

httpd-2.4.57-1.el9.x86_64.rpm

It contains:

  • Compiled binaries
  • Configuration files
  • Metadata (version, dependencies, description)
  • Scripts (pre/post install)

docs.title: 3. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
rpm [options] package

Examples:

rpm -ivh package.rpm
rpm -qa
rpm -qi bash

docs.title: 4.1 Install from a Local File (-i)
docs.text:
rpm -ivh httpd.rpm

Options:

  • -i → install
  • -v → verbose
  • -h → progress bar

docs.title: 4.2 Upgrade or Install (-U)
docs.text:
rpm -Uvh httpd.rpm

✔ Recommended over -i ✔ Upgrades if installed, installs if not

docs.title: 5. Remove an RPM Package
docs.text:
rpm -e httpd
  • -e → erase

Use package name, not file name.

docs.title: 6.1 List All Installed Packages
docs.text:
rpm -qa

Options:

  • -q → query
  • -a → all

docs.title: 6.2 Query a Specific Package
docs.text: rpm -qi bash

docs.title: 6.3 List Files Installed by a Package
docs.text: rpm -ql bash

docs.title: 6.4 Find Which Package Owns a File
docs.text: rpm -qf /bin/ls

docs.title: Package Info
docs.text: rpm -qip nginx.rpm

docs.title: List Files in an RPM File
docs.text: rpm -qlp nginx.rpm

docs.title: Show Required Dependencies
docs.text: rpm -qpR nginx.rpm

docs.title: Show What a Package Provides
docs.text: rpm -q --provides bash

docs.title: 9. Verify Installed Packages
docs.text:
rpm -V bash

Checks:

  • File size
  • Permissions
  • Ownership
  • Checksum

No output = everything OK.

docs.title: 10. Common rpm Options Summary
docs.text: Option Meaning -i Install -U Upgrade or install -e erase -q query -a all packages -l List files -f File owner -p Query RPM file -V Verify -h Progress bar

docs.title: Install Software from RPM
docs.text:
rpm -Uvh app.rpm

If dependency error appears:

failed dependencies:

➡️ Use yum / dnf instead.

docs.title: Check Installation
docs.text:
rpm -qi app
rpm -ql app

docs.title: Remove Software
docs.text: rpm -e app

docs.title: 12. rpm vs yum / dnf
docs.text:
Feature rpm yum / dnf Dependency resolution ❌ No ✅ Yes Install from repo ❌ No ✅ Yes Install local file ✅ Yes ✅ Yes Low-level control ✅ Yes ❌ Limited

📌 Use rpm to inspect packages 📌 Use yum / dnf to install software

docs.title: ❌ Using RPM File Name When Removing
docs.text: rpm -e httpd.rpm   # WRONG

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: rpm -e httpd

docs.title: ❌ Ignoring Dependencies
docs.text:
rpm -ivh app.rpm

Fails if dependencies are missing.

docs.title: Check if a Package Is Installed
docs.text: rpm -q openssh

docs.title: Find Config Files
docs.text: rpm -qc httpd

docs.title: Find Documentation Files
docs.text: rpm -qd bash

docs.title: 15. Quick Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
rpm -qa
rpm -qi pkg
rpm -ql pkg
rpm -qf file
rpm -Uvh pkg.rpm
rpm -e pkg --nodeps

docs.title: 16. Summary
docs.text:
• rpm is a low-level package manager
  • Works with .rpm files
  • Does not resolve dependencies
  • Best used for querying and inspecting packages
  • Prefer yum / dnf for real installations

docs.title: 1. What Is /etc/profile.d/?
docs.text:
/etc/profile.d/ is a directory that contains shell scripts.

These scripts are:

  • Automatically executed when a user logs in
  • Applied system-wide (for all users)
  • Used to set environment variables, PATH, aliases, etc.

📌 In CentOS (and RHEL-based systems), /etc/profile.d/ is sourced by /etc/profile.

docs.title: 2. When Are Scripts in /etc/profile.d/ Executed?
docs.text:
For login shells, the execution order is roughly:

  1. /etc/profile
  2. All *.sh scripts in /etc/profile.d/
  3. User’s ~/.bash_profile (or ~/.bash_login, ~/.profile)

So:

/etc/profile.d/*.sh runs before user-specific config

docs.title: 3. Why /etc/profile.d/ Exists
docs.text:
Without /etc/profile.d/, all system-wide settings would have to be placed in one file:

/etc/profile

That would be messy.

Instead:

  • Each application adds its own script
  • Configuration is modular and clean

Examples:

  • java.sh
  • maven.sh
  • go.sh

docs.title: 4. What Kind of Files Go in /etc/profile.d/?
✔ Allowed
docs.text:
• Shell scripts (.sh)
  • Environment variable definitions
  • PATH updates
  • Aliases and functions

docs.title: ❌ Not Recommended
docs.text:
• Heavy logic
  • Long-running commands
  • Interactive commands (read, vim, echo spam)

docs.title: 5. File Naming Rules
docs.text:
• Must end with .sh
  • Must be readable
  • Usually owned by root

Example:

/etc/profile.d/java.sh

docs.title: 6.1 Create Script (Root Required)
docs.text:
sudo vim /etc/profile.d/java.sh
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17
export PATH=$JAVA_HOME/bin:$PATH

docs.title: 6.2 Apply Changes
docs.text:
Log out and log in again, or:

source /etc/profile

Check:

echo $JAVA_HOME
java -version

docs.title: 7. Example: Add a Custom PATH
docs.text:
sudo vim /etc/profile.d/tools.sh
export PATH=/opt/tools/bin:$PATH

docs.title: 8. Example: Define Aliases
docs.text:
sudo vim /etc/profile.d/aliases.sh
alias ll='ls -lh'
alias gs='git status'

After login:

ll
gs

docs.title: 9. Which Shells Use /etc/profile.d/?
docs.text:
Shell Supported bash ✅ Yes sh ✅ Yes zsh ❌ No (by default) fish ❌ No

📌 /etc/profile.d/ works only for shells that read /etc/profile.

docs.title: Login Shell (Works)
docs.text:
ssh user@host
su - user

docs.title: Non-Login Shell (Does NOT run /etc/profile.d/)
docs.text:
bash

To apply settings:

source /etc/profile

docs.title: 11. /etc/profile.d/ vs Other Config Files
docs.text: File Scope /etc/profile System-wide, login /etc/profile.d/*.sh System-wide, modular ~/.bash_profile User login ~/.bashrc Interactive shell /etc/bashrc System-wide interactive

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting .sh Extension
docs.text:
java

Script won’t be loaded.

docs.title: ❌ Using echo or read
docs.text:
echo "Welcome"
read name

Breaks login experience.

docs.title: ❌ Overwriting PATH
docs.text: export PATH=/my/bin

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: export PATH=/my/bin:$PATH

docs.title: Check Which Scripts Are Loaded
docs.text: ls /etc/profile.d/

docs.title: Manually Test Script
docs.text: source /etc/profile.d/java.sh

docs.title: Check Login Shell
docs.text: echo $0

docs.title: 14. Practical Use Cases
docs.text:
• System-wide Java / Maven / Go setup
  • Company-wide environment variables
  • Teaching lab environments
  • Shared servers

docs.title: 15. Summary
docs.text:
• /etc/profile.d/ stores system-wide login shell scripts
  • Files must end with .sh
  • Executed for all users
  • Best place for global environment variables
  • Cleaner than editing /etc/profile directly

docs.title: CentOS Notes
docs.text: • 毕业实习使用的CentOS版本：CentOS-7.5-x86_64-DVD-1804.iso

docs.title: CentOS: Net
docs.text: Understanding Network Services in CentOS

docs.title: 1. What Are “Network Services”?
docs.text:
In CentOS, a network service is a background program (daemon) that:

  • Manages network connections
  • Provides network functionality
  • Starts automatically in the background

Examples:

  • network → basic network interface management
  • NetworkManager → dynamic network management
  • sshd → SSH remote login
  • firewalld → firewall service

docs.title: 2. Service Management Basics (systemd)
docs.text: CentOS 7 uses systemd to manage services.

docs.title: 2.1 Common systemctl Commands
docs.text:
systemctl status service_name
systemctl start service_name
systemctl stop service_name
systemctl restart service_name
systemctl enable service_name
systemctl disable service_name

Example:

sudo systemctl status network

docs.title: 3.1 What Is the network Service?
docs.text:
The network service:

  • Reads configuration files from /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/
  • Brings interfaces up or down
  • Uses files like ifcfg-ens33

Service name:

network

docs.title: 3.2 Check Network Service Status
docs.text: systemctl status network

docs.title: 3.3 Restart Network Service
docs.text:
sudo systemctl restart network

⚠️ Restarting the network service may disconnect SSH.

docs.title: 4.1 What Is NetworkManager?
docs.text:
NetworkManager is a more advanced network service that:

  • Supports Wi-Fi, VPN, mobile networks
  • Handles dynamic network changes
  • Is enabled by default in many systems

Service name:

NetworkManager

docs.title: 4.2 Check NetworkManager Status
docs.text: systemctl status NetworkManager

docs.title: 4.3 NetworkManager Tools
docs.text:
Tool Purpose nmcli Command-line management nmtui Text-based UI

Example:

nmtui

docs.title: 5. network vs NetworkManager
docs.text:
Item network NetworkManager Config style ifcfg files dynamic Server usage Common Common Desktop usage Rare Common DHCP support Yes Yes Recommended for beginners Yes Yes

⚠️ Do not manage the same interface with both at the same time.

docs.title: 6.1 What Is SSH?
docs.text:
SSH allows you to:

  • Log in remotely
  • Execute commands securely

Service name:

sshd

docs.title: 6.2 Check SSH Service
docs.text:
systemctl status sshd

Start SSH:

sudo systemctl start sshd
sudo systemctl enable sshd

docs.title: 7.1 What Is firewalld?
docs.text:
firewalld controls:

  • Which ports are open
  • Which services are allowed

Service name:

firewalld

docs.title: 7.2 Check Firewall Status
docs.text: systemctl status firewalld

docs.title: 7.3 Allow SSH Traffic
docs.text:
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=ssh --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

docs.title: 8.1 /etc/resolv.conf
docs.text:
Used for DNS:

cat /etc/resolv.conf

Example:

nameserver 8.8.8.8

This file is often managed automatically.

docs.title: 8.2 Name Service Switch (nsswitch.conf)
docs.text:
/etc/nsswitch.conf

Controls how the system resolves:

  • hostnames
  • users
  • groups

docs.title: 9. Common Network-Related Services Summary
docs.text: Service Purpose network Traditional network setup NetworkManager Dynamic network management sshd Remote login firewalld Firewall chronyd Time synchronization rsyslog Network logging

docs.title: 10. Checking Open Ports
docs.text:
ss -tuln

Or:

netstat -tuln

docs.title: 11. Typical Beginner Workflow
docs.text:
1. Configure ifcfg-ens33
  2. Restart network
  3. Verify IP and route
  4. Ensure sshd is running
  5. Open firewall ports if needed

docs.title: 12. Common Beginner Mistakes
docs.text: ❌ Restarting network over SSH without caution ❌ Disabling NetworkManager incorrectly ❌ Forgetting firewall rules ❌ Mixing network and NetworkManager

docs.title: 13. Troubleshooting Checklist
docs.text:
• systemctl status network
  • ip addr
  • ip route
  • ping gateway
  • ping 8.8.8.8
  • journalctl -u network

docs.title: 14. Summary
docs.text:
• Network services run in the background
  • Managed by systemctl
  • network and NetworkManager manage interfaces
  • sshd enables remote access
  • firewalld controls traffic

Understanding network services is essential for server administration 👍

docs.title: CentOS: ens33
docs.text: Understanding /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-ens33

docs.title: 1. What Is ifcfg-ens33?
docs.text:
In CentOS 7, network interfaces are configured using files located in:

/etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/

Each network interface has one configuration file:

ifcfg-<interface-name>

Example:

ifcfg-ens33

This file controls:

  • IP address
  • DHCP or static IP
  • Gateway
  • DNS
  • Whether the network starts at boot

docs.title: 2. What Is ens33?
docs.text:
ens33 is the network interface name.

Modern Linux uses predictable network interface names:

Name Meaning ens33 Ethernet + slot number eth0 Old-style naming lo Loopback interface

To list interfaces:

ip addr

docs.title: 3. When Is ifcfg-ens33 Used?
docs.text:
ifcfg-ens33 is used when:

  • CentOS boots
  • Network service restarts
  • Interface is brought up manually

The network service reads this file and configures the interface accordingly.

docs.title: 4. Typical ifcfg-ens33 File Example
docs.text:
TYPE=Ethernet
BOOTPROTO=dhcp
NAME=ens33
DEVICE=ens33
ONBOOT=yes

This is a DHCP-based configuration.

docs.title: 5.1 TYPE
docs.text:
TYPE=Ethernet
  • Network type
  • Usually Ethernet

docs.title: 5.2 BOOTPROTO
docs.text:
BOOTPROTO=dhcp

Controls how IP is obtained:

Value Meaning dhcp Automatically get IP none Static IP static Same as none

docs.title: 5.3 DEVICE
docs.text:
DEVICE=ens33
  • Interface name
  • Must match the real interface name

docs.title: 5.4 NAME
docs.text:
NAME=ens33
  • Human-readable name
  • Usually same as DEVICE

docs.title: 5.5 ONBOOT
docs.text:
ONBOOT=yes
  • Whether the interface starts at boot
  • yes is recommended for servers

docs.title: 6. Static IP Configuration (Very Important)
docs.text:
Example of a static IP configuration:

TYPE=Ethernet
BOOTPROTO=none
NAME=ens33
DEVICE=ens33
ONBOOT=yes

IPADDR=192.168.1.100
PREFIX=24
GATEWAY=192.168.1.1
DNS1=8.8.8.8
DNS2=1.1.1.1

docs.title: 7.1 IPADDR
docs.text:
IPADDR=192.168.1.100
  • The server’s IP address
  • Set this to your specific machine's IP

docs.title: 7.2 PREFIX
docs.text:
PREFIX=24
  • Subnet mask in CIDR format
  • 24 = 255.255.255.0

docs.title: 7.3 GATEWAY
docs.text:
GATEWAY=192.168.1.1
  • Default gateway
  • Required for internet access

docs.title: 7.4 DNS1, DNS2
docs.text:
DNS1=8.8.8.8
DNS2=1.1.1.1
  • DNS servers
  • Used for domain name resolution

docs.title: 8. Restart Network After Editing
docs.text:
After editing the file, restart the network:

sudo systemctl restart network

Or bring the interface down and up:

sudo ifdown ens33
sudo ifup ens33

docs.title: 9. How to Edit the File Safely
docs.text:
Use a text editor:

sudo vi /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-ens33

Before editing, make a backup:

sudo cp ifcfg-ens33 ifcfg-ens33.bak

docs.title: 10. Common Beginner Mistakes
docs.text: ❌ Interface name does not match ❌ ONBOOT=no ❌ Wrong gateway ❌ IP conflict on the network ❌ Forgetting to restart network

docs.title: 11. How to Check If It Works
11.1 Check IP
docs.text: ip addr show ens33

docs.title: 11.2 Check Routing
docs.text: ip route

docs.title: 11.3 Test Internet
docs.text:
ping 8.8.8.8
ping google.com

docs.title: 12. DHCP vs Static IP (Quick Comparison)
docs.text: Item DHCP Static IP assignment Automatic Manual Easy to use Yes No Server recommended No Yes Risk of IP change Yes No

docs.title: 13. Important Note (CentOS 8+)
docs.text:
In CentOS 8 and later:

  • network-scripts is deprecated
  • NetworkManager is used instead
  • Tools: nmcli, nmtui

But CentOS 7 still widely uses ifcfg-\* files.

docs.title: 14. Summary
docs.text:
• ifcfg-ens33 defines network settings for ens33
  • Located in /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/
  • Controls DHCP, static IP, DNS, gateway
  • Changes require network restart

If you understand this file, you understand basic Linux networking 👍

docs.title: CentOS: Hosts
docs.text: Understanding /etc/hosts

docs.title: 1. What Is /etc/hosts?
docs.text:
/etc/hosts is a local hostname resolution file.

In simple words:

It maps IP addresses to hostnames before the system asks a DNS server.

When your system tries to access a hostname (for example example.com), it:

  1. Checks /etc/hosts
  2. If not found, queries DNS

docs.title: 2. Why Does /etc/hosts Exist?
docs.text:
/etc/hosts is used for:

  • Quick hostname resolution
  • Testing without DNS
  • Local development
  • Temporary overrides
  • Small internal networks

It works without internet access.

docs.title: 3. Where Is /etc/hosts Located?
docs.text:
/etc/hosts

It is a plain text file.

docs.title: 4. Typical /etc/hosts File Example
docs.text:
127.0.0.1   localhost localhost.localdomain
::1         localhost localhost.localdomain

192.168.1.10  webserver
192.168.1.20  dbserver

docs.title: 5. Basic Format of /etc/hosts
docs.text:
Each line follows this format:

IP_address   hostname [alias1 alias2 ...]

Important rules:

  • Fields are separated by spaces or tabs
  • One IP can map to multiple names
  • Lines starting with # are comments

docs.title: 6.1 Loopback Address
docs.text:
127.0.0.1 localhost
  • Refers to your own machine
  • Used for local services

IPv6 equivalent:

::1 localhost

docs.title: 7. How /etc/hosts Works (Order Matters)
docs.text:
Linux hostname resolution order is defined in:

/etc/nsswitch.conf

Typical entry:

hosts: files dns

Meaning:

  1. files → /etc/hosts
  2. dns → DNS servers

So /etc/hosts overrides DNS.

docs.title: 8.1 Access a Server by Name
docs.text:
Instead of:

ssh 192.168.1.10

Use:

ssh webserver

docs.title: 8.2 Local Testing
docs.text:
127.0.0.1 mytest.com

Now:

ping mytest.com

resolves to 127.0.0.1.

docs.title: 8.3 Block a Website (Simple Trick)
docs.text:
127.0.0.1 facebook.com

The site will no longer load properly.

docs.title: 9. How to Edit /etc/hosts
docs.text: You need root privileges.

docs.title: 9.1 Edit with Vim
docs.text: sudo vi /etc/hosts

docs.title: 9.2 Backup First (Recommended)
docs.text: sudo cp /etc/hosts /etc/hosts.bak

docs.title: 10. Do Changes Take Effect Immediately?
docs.text:
Yes ✅

No restart is needed. Changes apply immediately.

Test with:

ping hostname

docs.title: 11. Common Beginner Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Typing wrong IP address ❌ Forgetting to save the file ❌ Using tabs inconsistently (spaces are fine) ❌ Expecting /etc/hosts to work across multiple machines

Remember:

/etc/hosts works only on the local machine.

docs.title: 12. /etc/hosts vs DNS (Quick Comparison)
docs.text: Item /etc/hosts DNS Scope Local Network-wide Easy to edit Yes No Scalable No Yes Common usage Testing Production

docs.title: 13. Security Note
docs.text:
• /etc/hosts can be abused by malware
  • Always review it if network behavior looks strange

Check file permissions:

ls -l /etc/hosts

docs.title: 14. Summary
docs.text:
• /etc/hosts maps IPs to names locally
  • Checked before DNS
  • Simple and powerful
  • Best for testing and small setups

If you understand /etc/hosts, you understand basic hostname resolution 👍

docs.title: RPM & yum
docs.text:
• Package Management Tool
  • RPM (Red Hat Package Manager, for Red Hat, CentOS, Fedora): yum (.rpm)
  • Debian system (like ubuntu): apt-get

docs.title: 1.1 RPM WHAT？
docs.text:
• RPM 是一种标准化的软件包管理格式和软件包管理工具。
  • 目的：提供一种标准的方式来打包、分发和安装软件。
  • 原理：RPM 包是一个存档文件，将软件及其相关文件、配置信息、依赖关系等打包成一个单独的RPM包，以 .rpm 为文件扩展名。
  • 意义：它使得在支持RPM的Linux发行版上安装、管理和分发软件变得更加方便和可靠。
  • 缺点：RPM 可以定义软件包之间的依赖关系，但不能解决软件包依赖性问题。

docs.title: 1.2 WHERE?
docs.text:
• Rpmfind

??? RPM包在Linux系统中常见的架构标识 * x86_64：表示64位x86架构，适用于使用64位处理器的计算机。 * i386、i686：表示32位x86架构，适用于使用32位处理器的计算机。 * armv6hl、armv7hl、aarch64：这些是ARM架构的不同变体。armv6hl适用于较旧的ARMv6架构，armv7hl适用于支持ARMv7指令集的设备，而aarch64适用于64位ARM架构。 * ppc64le：表示IBM Power架构的64位低能耗变体，适用于POWER8和POWER9处理器。 * s390x：表示IBM System z架构的64位变体，适用于IBM的大型机和主机系统。

  除了上述常见的架构标识外，还有一些其他特定架构的标识，如mips、mipsel、sparc等，它们在特定的硬件平台上使用。

  这些架构标识有助于确保选择适用于特定计算机架构的正确软件包，以确保兼容性和性能。

docs.title: 1.3 HOW?
docs.text:
# rpm
# RPM Package Manager.
# tags: [ packaging ]
# ---
# Show version of httpd package:
$ rpm -q httpd # (1)
# To install a package:
$ rpm -ivh <rpm> # (2)
# To remove a package:
$ rpm -e <package> # (3)

# For equivalent commands in other package managers, see <https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/Pacman/Rosetta>.
# More information: <https://rpm.org/>.
  1. q - query
  2. i - install; v - verbose; h - hash
  3. e - erase

docs.title: 2. yum
docs.text: Yum（Yellowdog Updater, Modified）是一个在Linux系统中常用的软件包管理工具，它可以帮助用户轻松地安装、更新和删除软件包。

docs.title: 2.1 yum WHAT？
docs.text:
• 基于 RPM 的简化软件包管理的工具。
  • 最初是为 Fedora 发行版开发的，现在也被许多其他 Linux 发行版所采用。
  • 会自动处理软件包之间的依赖关系，确保所需的其他软件包也会被正确地安装和配置。
  • 可以从预先配置的软件仓库中获取软件包。

docs.title: 2.2 HOW?
docs.text:
# yum
# Package management utility for RHEL, Fedora, and CentOS (for older versions).
# ---
# tags: [ packaging ]
# ---

# 准备工作
$ yum clean all
$ yum makecache
# Upgrade installed packages to the newest available versions:
$ yum upgrade

# To search for a package: 匹配名称和简介来搜索
$ yum search <package>

# 安装、更新、删除
# To install the latest version of a new package:
$ yum install <package> <package> <...>
# Install a new package and assume yes to all questions (also works with update, great for automated updates):
$ yum -y install package
# -y, --yes: 自动模式，对所有的提问都回答 yes
$ yum update <package>
# To remove a package:
$ yum remove <package>

# 描述和简介信息
# List packages matching <phrase>:
$ yum list <phrase>
$ yum list installed # 显示已装的软件包
$ yum list available # 显示可装（但未装）的软件包
# To find the dependencies of a package: 列出所有依赖关系
yum deplist <package>

# To find information about a package:
$ yum info <package>

# For equivalent commands in other package managers, see <https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/Pacman/Rosetta>.
# More information: <https://manned.org/yum>.

docs.title: 2.3 yum 的工作原理
docs.text:
• 软件仓库：提供软件包索引、信息（以供查询）和软件包（以供下载）

    Yum 通过与软件仓库进行交互来获取软件包信息。软件仓库是存储了大量软件包及其相关信息的服务器，用户可以从中获取需要的软件包。

  • 依赖解决：Yum 能够处理软件包之间的依赖关系。当用户安装一个软件包时，Yum 会自动检查并解决其所依赖的其他软件包。它会查找并安装所需的依赖包，以确保软件包能够正常运行。

  • 本地缓存：Yum 会在本地系统上建立一个缓存，将软件包的信息和文件保存在本地，利用本地缓存提高性能。这样，当用户再次需要同一软件包时，Yum 可以直接从本地缓存中获取，而无需再次从网络上下载。

  • 仓库配置：用户可以配置 Yum 以使用特定的软件仓库。配置文件中包含了软件仓库的地址、存储库的设置、软件包的优先级等信息。

docs.title: 2.4 配置 yum 的软件仓库
2.4.1 yum 常用的软件仓库及其介绍
2.4.2 CentOS 官方软件源特点
docs.text:
1. 不包括有版权争议的软件
  2. 软件包比较老旧
  3. 没有 eclipse、mplayer

docs.title: 2.4.2 /etc/yum.repos.d/
docs.text:
/etc 目录: 存放系统配置文件。

/etc/yum.repos.d/ 目录: 专门用于存放 Yum 软件仓库的配置文件。

配置文件命名规则：每个 Yum 软件仓库都有一个对应的配置文件，通常以 .repo 扩展名结尾。配置文件的命名规则一般为 <仓库名称>.repo，其中 <仓库名称> 是自定义的仓库标识符。

文件内容：每个配置文件包含仓库的名称、URL、GPG密钥、软件包信息等。这些信息定义了如何获取软件包、软件包的来源、安全验证等。配置文件使用INI格式，包括段落（section）和键值对（key-value）的形式。

多仓库支持：可以在 /etc/yum.repos.d/ 目录下配置多个仓库的配置文件，每个配置文件代表一个不同的软件仓库。这样可以方便地管理和切换不同的软件源，以获取所需的软件包。

管理方式：可以通过创建、编辑或删除 /etc/yum.repos.d/ 目录下的配置文件来管理 Yum 软件仓库。

$ cd /etc/yum.repos.d/
$ yum install yum-plugin-priorities

[RPM]: Redhat Package Manager [yum]: Yellowdog Updater, Modified

docs.title: 1. What is awk?
docs.text:
awk is a text processing and reporting tool designed to work with:

  • Structured text
  • Columns (fields)
  • Line-based records

It is especially good for:

  • Processing tables
  • Log analysis
  • Data extraction
  • Simple calculations

If:

  • sed = line editor
  • awk = mini programming language for text

docs.title: 2. When Should You Use awk?
docs.text:
Use awk when:

  • Data is column-based
  • You need conditions
  • You need calculations or statistics
  • You need formatted output

Example input:

Tom  18  85
Bob  20  90
Lucy 19  88

docs.title: 3. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
awk 'pattern { action }' file
  • pattern → condition (optional)
  • action → what to do (usually print)

If pattern is omitted → applies to all lines If action is omitted → default is { print }

docs.title: 4. Your First awk Command
docs.text:
Print entire file:

awk '{ print }' file.txt

Same as:

cat file.txt

docs.title: 5. Fields and Records (CORE CONCEPT)
docs.text:
• Each line = one record
  • Each column = one field

Default field separator: spaces or tabs

Variable Meaning $0 Entire line $1 First field $2 Second field NF Number of fields NR Line number

Example:

awk '{ print $1, $3 }' file.txt

docs.title: 6. Printing Selected Fields
docs.text:
Print name and score:

awk '{ print $1, $3 }' scores.txt

Print last field:

awk '{ print $NF }' file.txt

docs.title: 7. Using Conditions (Patterns)
docs.text:
Print lines where score > 85:

awk '$3 > 85 { print $1, $3 }' scores.txt

Print lines containing "ERROR":

awk '/ERROR/ { print }' log.txt

docs.title: 8. Built-in Variables
docs.text:
Variable Description NR Record number NF Field count FS Field separator OFS Output field separator RS Record separator ORS Output record separator

Print line number + content:

awk '{ print NR, $0 }' file.txt

docs.title: 9.1 Using -F
docs.text:
Comma-separated file:

awk -F ',' '{ print $1, $2 }' data.csv

Colon-separated:

awk -F ':' '{ print $1 }' /etc/passwd

docs.title: 9.2 Inside awk
docs.text: awk 'BEGIN { FS=":" } { print $1 }' /etc/passwd

docs.title: 10.1 BEGIN
docs.text:
Executed before reading input

awk 'BEGIN { print "Start processing" } { print $1 }' file.txt

docs.title: 10.2 END
docs.text:
Executed after all lines

awk '{ sum += $3 } END { print "Total:", sum }' scores.txt

docs.title: 11. Simple Calculations
docs.text:
Average score:

awk '{ sum += $3 } END { print sum/NR }' scores.txt

docs.title: 12. String Functions
docs.text:
Function Description length() String length tolower() Lowercase toupper() Uppercase substr() Substring index() Find position

Example:

awk '{ print toupper($1) }' file.txt

docs.title: 13. Conditions (if / else)
docs.text:
awk '{
  if ($3 >= 90)
    print $1, "Excellent"
  else
    print $1, "OK"
}' scores.txt

docs.title: for loop
docs.text:
awk '{
  for (i=1; i<=NF; i++)
    print $i
}' file.txt

docs.title: 15. Formatting Output
docs.text:
awk '{ printf "%-10s %5d\n", $1, $3 }' scores.txt
  • printf does not add newline automatically

docs.title: 16. Using awk with Pipes
docs.text:
ps aux | awk '{ print $1, $2 }'
ls -l | awk '{ print $9, $5 }'

docs.title: Sum file sizes
docs.text: ls -l | awk '{ sum += $5 } END { print sum }'

docs.title: Count lines
docs.text: awk 'END { print NR }' file.txt

docs.title: Remove duplicate lines
docs.text: awk '!seen[$0]++' file.txt

docs.title: Extract IP addresses
docs.text: awk '{ print $1 }' access.log

docs.title: 18. awk vs sed
docs.text: Feature sed awk Line editing Yes Limited Column handling No Yes Calculations No Yes Programming No Yes

docs.title: 19. Common awk Versions
docs.text:
• awk → system default
  • gawk → GNU awk (most common on Linux)
  • mawk → faster, lightweight

Check version:

awk --version

docs.title: 20. Summary
docs.text:
• awk is perfect for structured text
  • Think in terms of:
    • records (lines)
    • fields (columns)
  • Learn first:
    • $1, $2, $NF
    • NR, NF
    • BEGIN, END
    • conditions

docs.title: 1. What is grep?
docs.text:
grep stands for Global Regular Expression Print.

It is used to:

  • Search text for specific patterns
  • Print lines that match a keyword or pattern
  • Work with files, directories, and command output

grep is one of the most important and frequently used Linux commands.

docs.title: 2. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
grep [options] PATTERN FILE

Examples:

grep root /etc/passwd
grep error logfile.txt

docs.title: Search for a Word in a File
docs.text:
grep linux notes.txt

✔ Prints all lines in notes.txt that contain linux.

docs.title: Case Sensitivity
docs.text:
By default, grep is case-sensitive:

grep Linux notes.txt

docs.title: Ignore Case (-i)
docs.text:
grep -i linux notes.txt

Matches:

  • linux
  • Linux
  • LINUX

docs.title: 4. Searching Multiple Files
docs.text:
grep main *.c

Shows matching lines and file names.

docs.title: 5. Commonly Used Options (Beginner Must-Know)
docs.text: Option Meaning -i Ignore case -n Show line numbers -v Invert match (NOT matching) -r / -R Recursive search -l Show only file names -c Count matching lines -w Match whole word --color=auto Highlight matches

docs.title: 6. Show Line Numbers (-n)
docs.text:
grep -n error app.log

Output:

15:error: connection failed

docs.title: 7. Search for Lines That Do NOT Match (-v)
docs.text:
grep -v root /etc/passwd

✔ Shows all users except root.

docs.title: 8. Recursive Search in Directories (-r)
docs.text:
grep -r TODO .

Searches all files under the current directory.

docs.title: 9. Search Only File Names (-l)
docs.text:
grep -l main *.c

Output:

main.c
test.c

docs.title: 10. Count Matches (-c)
docs.text:
grep -c error app.log

Counts matching lines, not occurrences.

docs.title: 11. Match Whole Words (-w)
docs.text:
grep -w cat animals.txt

✔ Matches cat ❌ Does not match catch, category

docs.title: 12. Use grep with Pipes (|)
docs.text:
Very common usage:

ps aux | grep nginx
dmesg | grep -i usb
ls -l | grep '^d'

docs.title: 13.1 Beginning of Line (^)
docs.text: grep '^root' /etc/passwd

docs.title: 13.2 End of Line ($)
docs.text: grep 'bash$' /etc/passwd

docs.title: 13.3 Match Any Character (.)
docs.text:
grep 'h.t' file.txt

Matches:

  • hat
  • hit
  • hot

docs.title: 13.4 Character Classes
docs.text:
grep '[0-9]' file.txt

Matches lines containing digits.

docs.title: 14. grep Variants
docs.text:
Command Purpose grep Basic search egrep Extended regex (same as grep -E) fgrep Fixed strings (same as grep -F)

Examples:

grep -E 'error|fail' app.log
grep -F 'a+b' file.txt

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting Quotes
docs.text:
grep error|fail file.txt

Shell interprets |.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: grep 'error|fail' file.txt

docs.title: ❌ Searching Binary Files
docs.text:
grep text program

May produce garbage output.

docs.title: ✅ Better
docs.text: grep -I text program

docs.title: Find Failed SSH Logins
docs.text: grep "Failed password" /var/log/secure

docs.title: Search Configuration Files
docs.text: grep -r "Listen" /etc/httpd

docs.title: Find TODO Comments in Code
docs.text: grep -rn TODO .

docs.title: 17. Quick Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
grep word file
grep -i word file
grep -n word file
grep -v word file
grep -r word dir
grep -E 'a|b' file

docs.title: 18. Summary
docs.text:
• grep searches text using patterns
  • Extremely powerful when combined with pipes
  • Start with simple keywords, then learn regex
  • Essential for logs, configs, and debugging

docs.title: 1. What is sed?
docs.text:
sed stands for Stream Editor. It is a non-interactive text processing tool used to:

  • Search and replace text
  • Insert, delete, or modify lines
  • Transform text in pipelines
  • Process large files efficiently

sed works line by line, reading input from:

  • Files
  • Standard input (pipes)

It does not modify files by default — it prints results to standard output.

docs.title: 2. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
sed 'command' file

or with pipes:

command | sed 'command'

Example:

sed 'p' file.txt

docs.title: 3. How sed Works (Core Idea)
docs.text:
For each line:

  1. Read one line into pattern space
  2. Apply the command(s)
  3. Print the result (unless suppressed)
  4. Move to the next line

docs.title: 4.1 Print Every Line (Default)
docs.text:
sed '' file.txt

Same as:

cat file.txt

docs.title: 4.2 Print Specific Lines
docs.text:
Use -n to suppress automatic output.

sed -n 'p' file.txt

Print line 3:

sed -n '3p' file.txt

Print lines 3 to 5:

sed -n '3,5p' file.txt

Print the last line:

sed -n '$p' file.txt

docs.title: 5. Deleting Lines
docs.text:
Delete line 3:

sed '3d' file.txt

Delete lines 3–5:

sed '3,5d' file.txt

Delete empty lines:

sed '/^$/d' file.txt

docs.title: 6.1 Basic Substitution
docs.text:
sed 's/old/new/' file.txt
  • Replaces first match per line

Example:

sed 's/apple/orange/' file.txt

docs.title: 6.2 Global Replacement
docs.text:
sed 's/old/new/g' file.txt

Replace all matches in each line.

docs.title: 6.3 Replace on Specific Lines
docs.text:
Only on line 3:

sed '3s/old/new/' file.txt

Only on lines 3–5:

sed '3,5s/old/new/g' file.txt

docs.title: 6.4 Replace Lines Matching a Pattern
docs.text: sed '/ERROR/s/old/new/' file.txt

docs.title: 7.1 Common Regex Symbols
docs.text:
Symbol Meaning ^ Line start $ Line end . Any character * Zero or more .* Anything [] Character set

Example: Replace numbers

sed 's/[0-9]/X/g' file.txt

docs.title: 7.2 Anchors
docs.text:
Replace lines starting with root:

sed 's/^root/USER/' file.txt

Replace lines ending with .log:

sed 's/\.log$/\.bak/' file.txt

docs.title: 8.1 Insert Before a Line
docs.text: sed '3i\NEW LINE' file.txt

docs.title: 8.2 Append After a Line
docs.text: sed '3a\NEW LINE' file.txt

docs.title: 8.3 Change a Line
docs.text: sed '3c\THIS LINE IS REPLACED' file.txt

docs.title: 9. Working with Files (In-Place Editing)
docs.text:
⚠️ Be careful — this modifies the file.

sed -i 's/old/new/g' file.txt

On macOS (BSD sed):

sed -i '' 's/old/new/g' file.txt

Create a backup:

sed -i.bak 's/old/new/g' file.txt

docs.title: 10.1 Using ;
docs.text: sed 's/foo/bar/g; s/hello/hi/g' file.txt

docs.title: 10.2 Using -e
docs.text: sed -e 's/foo/bar/g' -e 's/hello/hi/g' file.txt

docs.title: 11. Using sed with Pipes
docs.text:
Replace spaces with commas:

ls | sed 's/ /,/g'

Filter and transform:

ps aux | sed -n '1,5p'

docs.title: Remove comments
docs.text: sed 's/#.*//' file.conf

docs.title: Trim leading spaces
docs.text: sed 's/^[ \t]*//' file.txt

docs.title: Trim trailing spaces
docs.text: sed 's/[ \t]*$//' file.txt

docs.title: Number lines
docs.text: sed = file.txt | sed 'N; s/\n/\t/'

docs.title: 13. GNU sed vs BSD sed (Important!)
docs.text:
Feature GNU sed (Linux) BSD sed (macOS) -i sed -i sed -i '' Regex More features Slightly limited

Always test commands before using -i.

docs.title: 14. When to Use sed
docs.text:
Use sed when:

  • You need fast, scriptable text processing
  • You work with pipelines
  • You modify large files

Avoid sed when:

  • You need complex logic → use awk or Python

docs.title: 15. Summary
docs.text:
• sed is a powerful stream editor
  • Best for search/replace, line editing, pipelines
  • Learn these first:
    • s///
    • p, d
    • -n
    • regex basics

docs.title: set
docs.text:
The set command in Linux is a built-in shell command (works in Bash, Zsh, etc.) that can:

  1. Display shell variables and functions
  2. Set shell options
  3. Control shell behavior

It is a very powerful tool for shell scripting and debugging.

docs.title: 1.1 View all shell variables and functions
docs.text:
set
  • Running set without arguments lists all shell variables and functions in the current shell.
  • Example:
$ set
BASH=/bin/bash
HOME=/home/user
PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
...
my_function () {
    echo "Hello"
}

Tip: set lists variables alphabetically, unlike env or printenv, which only show environment variables.

docs.title: 1.2 Set a shell variable
docs.text:
set VARIABLE_NAME=value
  • Example:
set MY_VAR="Hello"
echo $MY_VAR
# Output: Hello

Note: In Bash, it’s more common to just use MY_VAR="Hello" without set. Using set is optional for simple variables.

docs.title: 2. set Options (Shell Behavior)
docs.text: You can use set with flags to control how the shell behaves.

docs.title: 2.1 Enable debugging: -x
docs.text:
set -x
  • Prints each command before executing it.
  • Useful for debugging scripts.
#!/bin/bash
set -x
echo "Hello"
VAR=5
echo $VAR

Output:

+ echo 'Hello'
Hello
+ VAR=5
+ echo 5
5

docs.title: 2.2 Disable debugging: +x
docs.text: set +x

docs.title: 2.3 Treat unset variables as errors: -u
docs.text:
set -u
echo $UNDEFINED_VAR
  • If UNDEFINED_VAR is not set, the shell will exit with an error.
  • Helps catch typos and mistakes in scripts.

docs.title: 2.4 Exit on error: -e
docs.text:
set -e
  • Makes the shell exit immediately if a command fails (returns non-zero).
  • Example:
set -e
ls /not_exist_dir
echo "This will not print"

docs.title: 2.5 Combine multiple options
docs.text:
set -eux
  • -e → exit on error
  • -u → treat unset variables as errors
  • -x → debug mode (print commands)
  • Useful for robust scripting.

docs.title: 3. Stop all options: set +o
docs.text:
set +o
  • Displays all shell options with on/off status.

docs.title: 3.1 Turn off a specific option
docs.text:
set +e  # stop exiting on error
set +u  # stop error on unset variable

docs.title: 3.2 Turn on a specific option
docs.text:
set -o errexit  # same as set -e
set -o nounset  # same as set -u
set -o xtrace   # same as set -x

docs.title: 4. Common Shell Options (for Beginners)
docs.text: Option Shortcut Description errexit -e Exit script if a command fails nounset -u Treat unset variables as errors xtrace -x Print each command before executing noglob -f Disable pathname expansion (wildcards) ignoreeof none Prevent Ctrl+D from exiting shell

docs.title: 5. Summary
docs.text:
• set without arguments → show all shell variables/functions.
  • set VARIABLE=value → set a shell variable.
  • set -x → debug mode.
  • set -e → exit on error.
  • set -u → unset variables cause errors.
  • set +o → list shell options.

Tips for beginners:

  • Use set -eux in scripts to catch errors early.
  • Use set +x to stop debug output when not needed.
  • Remember: set changes only the current shell session, unless used in scripts.

docs.title: 1. What is source?
docs.text:
source is a shell built-in command used to:

👉 Read and execute commands from a file in the current shell environment

This is very important:

Commands run by source affect your current shell, unlike running a script normally, which starts a new shell.

docs.title: 2. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
source filename

or the shorter form:

. filename

Both are exactly the same.

docs.title: 3. Why source Is Important
docs.text:
Normally, when you run a script:

./script.sh
  • It runs in a subshell
  • Environment changes (variables, cd, export) do not affect your current shell

But with source:

source script.sh
  • It runs in the current shell
  • Changes remain after execution

docs.title: 4.1 Create a Script
docs.text:
vim test.sh
#!/bin/bash
MYVAR="hello"
export MYVAR

docs.title: 4.2 Run Normally (Subshell)
docs.text:
./test.sh
echo $MYVAR

Output:

(empty)

docs.title: 4.3 Run with source
docs.text:
source test.sh
echo $MYVAR

Output:

hello

✔ The variable exists because it was set in the current shell.

docs.title: 5. source vs ./script.sh
docs.text: Method Runs in Environment Changed? ./script.sh Subshell ❌ No bash script.sh Subshell ❌ No source script.sh Current shell ✅ Yes . script.sh Current shell ✅ Yes

docs.title: 6.1 Reload Shell Configuration
docs.text:
After editing .bashrc:

vim ~/.bashrc

Apply changes immediately:

source ~/.bashrc

Without this, you would need to open a new terminal.

docs.title: 6.2 Load Environment Variables
docs.text:
File: env.sh

export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17
export PATH=$JAVA_HOME/bin:$PATH

Load it:

source env.sh

docs.title: 6.3 Activate Python Virtual Environment
docs.text:
source venv/bin/activate

This works only with source, not ./activate.

docs.title: 6.4 Define Functions and Aliases
docs.text:
# tools.sh
myll() {
    ls -lh
}

alias gs='git status'

Load them:

source tools.sh

docs.title: 7. The . (Dot) Command
docs.text:
This is the POSIX-compatible form:

. filename

Example:

. ~/.bash_profile

✔ Works in sh, bash, and other shells.

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting File Path
docs.text:
source test.sh

If the file is in the current directory, this may fail.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: source ./test.sh

docs.title: ❌ Expecting cd to Persist Without source
docs.text:
./change_dir.sh
pwd   # directory unchanged

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
source change_dir.sh
pwd   # directory changed

docs.title: ❌ Sourcing Untrusted Scripts
docs.text:
source unknown.sh

⚠️ This can be dangerous, because it runs with your current shell permissions.

docs.title: 9. When NOT to Use source
docs.text:
• Running long automation scripts
  • Running untrusted scripts
  • Scripts that should not affect your environment

Use normal execution instead:

./script.sh

docs.title: 10. Practical Example: Config File Pattern
docs.text:
# config.sh
DB_HOST=localhost
DB_PORT=3306

Main script:

source config.sh
echo "$DB_HOST:$DB_PORT"

docs.title: 11. Summary
docs.text:
• source executes a file in the current shell
  • Changes to variables, functions, aliases persist
  • . filename is equivalent to source filename
  • Commonly used for:
    • Shell config files
    • Environment variables
    • Virtual environment activation

docs.title: 1. What is systemctl?
docs.text:
systemctl is the main command-line tool used to control and manage services on Linux systems that use systemd.

In simple terms:

  • service = a background program (e.g. ssh, nginx, mysql)
  • systemd = the system that starts, stops, and monitors those services
  • systemctl = the tool you use to talk to systemd

Most modern Linux distributions use systemd, including:

  • Ubuntu (16.04+)
  • Debian (8+)
  • CentOS / Rocky / Alma
  • Arch Linux
  • Raspberry Pi OS

docs.title: 2. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
systemctl [OPTIONS] COMMAND [UNIT]
  • COMMAND: what you want to do (start, stop, status, …)
  • UNIT: the target service (e.g. ssh, nginx, mysql)

Example:

systemctl status ssh

docs.title: 3. Understanding “Units”
docs.text:
In systemd, everything is called a unit.

Common unit types:

Type Suffix Meaning Service .service Background service Target .target Group of units Mount .mount Mount point Timer .timer Scheduled task (cron-like)

👉 Usually, you can omit the suffix:

systemctl status ssh
# same as
systemctl status ssh.service

docs.title: Check if a service is running
docs.text:
systemctl status nginx

You will see:

  • Active / inactive
  • Running / failed
  • Recent logs
  • PID and resource usage

Key status lines:

Active: active (running)
Active: inactive (dead)
Active: failed

docs.title: Start a service
docs.text: sudo systemctl start nginx

docs.title: Stop a service
docs.text: sudo systemctl stop nginx

docs.title: Restart a service
docs.text: sudo systemctl restart nginx

docs.title: Reload configuration (if supported)
docs.text:
sudo systemctl reload nginx

🔹 restart = stop + start 🔹 reload = reread config without stopping

docs.title: 6. Enable vs Start (Very Important!)
docs.text:
This is a common beginner confusion.

Command Meaning start Start now enable Start at boot

docs.title: Enable service at boot
docs.text: sudo systemctl enable mysql

docs.title: Disable service at boot
docs.text: sudo systemctl disable mysql

docs.title: Enable and start immediately
docs.text: sudo systemctl enable --now mysql

docs.title: List all running services
docs.text: systemctl list-units --type=service

docs.title: List all installed services (including disabled)
docs.text: systemctl list-unit-files --type=service

docs.title: Show only failed services
docs.text: systemctl --failed

docs.title: 8. Viewing Logs with journalctl
docs.text: systemd uses a centralized logging system.

docs.title: View logs for a service
docs.text: journalctl -u nginx

docs.title: Follow logs in real time
docs.text: journalctl -u nginx -f

docs.title: View logs since boot
docs.text: journalctl -u nginx -b

docs.title: Check current default target
docs.text:
systemctl get-default

Common targets:

  • multi-user.target → server mode (no GUI)
  • graphical.target → desktop mode

docs.title: Change default target
docs.text: sudo systemctl set-default multi-user.target

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting sudo
docs.text:
Most service operations require root:

sudo systemctl restart ssh

docs.title: ❌ Mixing service and systemctl
docs.text:
Older systems used:

service nginx restart

Modern systems should use:

systemctl restart nginx

docs.title: ❌ Confusing enable and start
docs.text:
• enable does not start immediately
  • start does not persist across reboot

docs.title: 11. Useful Shortcuts
docs.text: Task Command Check if service is active systemctl is-active ssh Check if enabled at boot systemctl is-enabled ssh Mask a service (fully block) sudo systemctl mask service Unmask a service sudo systemctl unmask service

docs.title: 12. When Should You Use systemctl?
docs.text:
You will use systemctl when:

  • Managing web servers (nginx, apache)
  • Managing databases (mysql, postgresql)
  • Managing SSH
  • Debugging boot issues
  • Writing server automation scripts

docs.title: 13. Summary
docs.text:
• systemctl is the control center for services on modern Linux
  • Learn these first:
    • status
    • start / stop / restart
    • enable / disable
  • Understand the difference between now and at boot
  • Combine with journalctl for debugging

docs.title: 1. What is tar?
docs.text:
tar stands for Tape Archive. It is a classic Linux/Unix command used to:

  • Pack multiple files/directories into one file (archive)
  • Extract files from an archive
  • Often used together with compression tools like gzip, bzip2, or xz

Common file extensions:

  • .tar → archive only
  • .tar.gz or .tgz → tar + gzip
  • .tar.bz2 → tar + bzip2
  • .tar.xz → tar + xz

docs.title: 2. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
tar [options] archive_name files_or_directories

Example:

tar -cf archive.tar file1 file2 dir1

docs.title: 3. Most Important Options (Must Know)
docs.text:
Option Meaning -c Create an archive -x Extract an archive -t List contents of an archive -f Specify archive file name -v Verbose (show progress) -z Use gzip compression -j Use bzip2 compression -J Use xz compression -C Extract to a directory

⚠️ Important: -f must be followed immediately by the archive name

docs.title: 4.1 Archive a Single File
docs.text: tar -cf file.tar file.txt

docs.title: 4.2 Archive Multiple Files
docs.text: tar -cf files.tar a.txt b.txt c.txt

docs.title: 4.3 Archive a Directory
docs.text:
tar -cf project.tar project/

This includes all subdirectories and files.

docs.title: 4.4 Show Progress While Creating (-v)
docs.text: tar -cvf project.tar project/

docs.title: 5.1 Extract a .tar File
docs.text: tar -xf project.tar

docs.title: 5.2 Extract with Verbose Output
docs.text: tar -xvf project.tar

docs.title: 5.3 Extract to a Specific Directory
docs.text:
tar -xf project.tar -C /tmp

The directory must already exist.

docs.title: 6. Listing Archive Contents (Without Extracting)
docs.text:
tar -tf project.tar

With details:

tar -tvf project.tar

docs.title: 7.1 Create a .tar.gz (Most Common)
docs.text:
tar -czvf project.tar.gz project/

Breakdown:

  • -c create
  • -z gzip
  • -v verbose
  • -f file

docs.title: 7.2 Extract a .tar.gz
docs.text: tar -xzvf project.tar.gz

docs.title: 7.3 Create a .tar.bz2
docs.text:
tar -cjvf project.tar.bz2 project/

Extract:

tar -xjvf project.tar.bz2

docs.title: 7.4 Create a .tar.xz (Best Compression)
docs.text:
tar -cJvf project.tar.xz project/

Extract:

tar -xJvf project.tar.xz

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting -f
docs.text: tar -cv project.tar project/   # WRONG

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: tar -cvf project.tar project/

docs.title: ❌ Wrong Order (Archive name not after -f)
docs.text: tar -cf project/ project.tar   # WRONG

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: tar -cf project.tar project/

docs.title: Backup /etc Directory
docs.text: tar -czvf etc-backup.tar.gz /etc

docs.title: Extract Only One File from Archive
docs.text: tar -xvf project.tar README.md

docs.title: Check Archive Before Extracting
docs.text: tar -tf project.tar

docs.title: 10. Quick Reference Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
# Create tar
tar -cf archive.tar dir/

# Extract tar
tar -xf archive.tar

# Create tar.gz
tar -czvf archive.tar.gz dir/

# Extract tar.gz
tar -xzvf archive.tar.gz

# List contents
tar -tf archive.tar

docs.title: 11. Summary
docs.text:
• tar is for archiving, not compression (compression is optional)
  • -c create, -x extract, -t list
  • -f is mandatory
  • Compression options: -z, -j, -J
  • Widely used for backups, source code, and software distribution

docs.title: test
docs.text:
# Test if a file exists:
test -f "path/to/file_or_directory"

docs.title: 1. What is xargs?
docs.text:
xargs means “extended arguments”.

It is used to:

👉 Take input from standard input (stdin) and turn it into command arguments

In simple words:

xargs connects commands that output text with commands that require arguments.

docs.title: 2. Why Do We Need xargs?
docs.text:
Many commands output text, but other commands need arguments.

Example problem:

find . -name "*.log" | rm

❌ This does not work because rm needs file names as arguments.

Solution:

find . -name "*.log" | xargs rm

✔ xargs passes the file names to rm.

docs.title: 3. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
command1 | xargs command2

Example:

echo "a b c" | xargs rm

Equivalent to:

rm a b c

docs.title: Print Words on One Line
docs.text:
echo "hello world linux" | xargs

Output:

hello world linux

docs.title: Convert Lines to Arguments
docs.text:
printf "a
b
c
" | xargs echo

Output:

a b c

docs.title: 5.1 Remove Files
docs.text:
ls *.tmp | xargs rm

Better (safer):

ls *.tmp | xargs rm -v

docs.title: 5.2 Count Lines in Many Files
docs.text: ls *.txt | xargs wc -l

docs.title: 5.3 Search Inside Files
docs.text: grep -l error *.log | xargs less

docs.title: 6. Important Options (Beginner Must-Know)
docs.text: Option Meaning -n N Use N arguments per command -I {} Replace placeholder -0 Input items are separated by NUL -p Prompt before execution -t Print command before executing

docs.title: 7. Limit Arguments per Command (-n)
docs.text:
echo "1 2 3 4" | xargs -n 2 echo

Output:

1 2
3 4

docs.title: 8. Use Placeholder (-I {})
docs.text:
ls *.txt | xargs -I {} cp {} backup/

✔ {} represents each input item.

docs.title: 9. Handle Spaces in File Names (Very Important!)
❌ Problem
docs.text:
ls | xargs rm

Fails for:

my file.txt

docs.title: ✅ Correct Way (-print0 + -0)
docs.text:
find . -name "*.tmp" -print0 | xargs -0 rm

This is the safest way.

docs.title: 10. Ask Before Executing (-p)
docs.text:
ls *.log | xargs -p rm

You will be asked for confirmation.

docs.title: 11. Show Commands Before Running (-t)
docs.text:
ls *.log | xargs -t rm

Output:

rm a.log b.log

docs.title: Using a Loop
docs.text:
for f in *.txt; do
    wc -l "$f"
done

docs.title: Using xargs
docs.text:
ls *.txt | xargs wc -l

✔ Shorter and often faster.

docs.title: 13. When NOT to Use xargs
docs.text:
• When the command already supports multiple files directly
  • When filenames may contain newlines (unless using -0)
  • When clarity is more important than brevity

docs.title: Kill Processes by Name
docs.text:
ps aux | grep nginx | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kill

(Safer alternative exists, but this shows the idea.)

docs.title: Change Permissions
docs.text: find . -type f -name "*.sh" | xargs chmod +x

docs.title: Compress Many Files
docs.text: ls *.log | xargs tar -czvf logs.tar.gz

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting File Safety
docs.text: ls | xargs rm

docs.title: ✅ Safer
docs.text: find . -type f -print0 | xargs -0 rm

docs.title: ❌ Using xargs When Not Needed
docs.text:
rm $(ls *.tmp)

This may break for many files.

docs.title: 16. Quick Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
cmd | xargs cmd2
xargs -n 1 cmd
xargs -I {} cmd {}
xargs -0 cmd
xargs -p cmd

docs.title: 17. Summary
docs.text:
• xargs converts stdin into command arguments
  • Very powerful with pipes
  • Be careful with spaces and special characters
  • Use -print0 and -0 for safety

docs.title: 1. What is curl?
docs.text:
curl (Client URL) is a command-line tool for transferring data between your computer and a server. It supports many protocols, including HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SFTP, and more.

Typical use cases:

  • Test web APIs
  • Send HTTP requests (GET / POST)
  • Download or upload files
  • Debug web services

docs.title: 2. Check if curl is installed
docs.text:
Most Linux distributions include curl by default.

curl --version

If not installed:

docs.title: Ubuntu / Debian
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install curl

docs.title: CentOS / Rocky / AlmaLinux
docs.text: sudo yum install curl

docs.title: Fetch a web page (GET request)
docs.text:
curl https://example.com

The HTML content is printed directly to the terminal.

docs.title: Save output to a file
docs.text:
curl -O https://example.com/file.zip

(-O keeps the original filename)

curl -o myfile.zip https://example.com/file.zip

(-o lets you rename the file)

docs.title: 4. Show download progress
docs.text:
curl -O https://example.com/file.zip

By default, curl shows a progress bar.

Silent mode:

curl -s https://example.com

docs.title: 5. Follow redirects
docs.text:
Many URLs redirect automatically.

curl -L https://example.com

(-L follows HTTP 301 / 302 redirects)

docs.title: Show response headers
docs.text: curl -I https://example.com

docs.title: Show full request + response (debug)
docs.text: curl -v https://example.com

docs.title: Send GET request with query parameters
docs.text: curl "https://api.example.com/search?q=linux&page=1"

docs.title: Send POST request
docs.text: curl -X POST https://api.example.com/login

docs.title: Send JSON data
docs.text:
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/login \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"username":"user","password":"123456"}'

docs.title: HTTP Basic Auth
docs.text:
curl -u username:password https://example.com/protected

⚠️ Passwords may be saved in shell history.

docs.title: 9. Upload files
docs.text: curl -F "file=@test.txt" https://example.com/upload

docs.title: 10. Limit download speed
docs.text: curl --limit-rate 500k -O https://example.com/file.iso

docs.title: 11. Common options summary
docs.text: Option Meaning -o Output to file (rename) -O Save with original name -L Follow redirects -I Headers only -X HTTP method -H Add HTTP header -d Send data -u Authentication -v Verbose mode

docs.title: 12. curl vs wget
docs.text:
curl wget Best for APIs Best for file downloads Supports many protocols Focused on downloads Fine-grained control Recursive downloads

👉 Rule of thumb: Use curl for APIs and debugging, wget for bulk downloads.

docs.title: 13. Practical examples
docs.text:
curl https://example.com
curl -I https://example.com
curl -L -O https://example.com/app.tar.gz
curl -X POST -d "name=tom&age=18" https://example.com/form

docs.title: 14. Get help
docs.text:
curl --help
man curl

docs.title: ✅ Summary
docs.text:
• curl is a powerful tool for interacting with web services
  • Essential for API testing and debugging
  • Widely used in DevOps, backend, and scripting

docs.title: 1. What is scp?
docs.text:
scp stands for Secure Copy.

It is used to:

  • Copy files and directories between computers
  • Work over SSH
  • Encrypt data during transfer

📌 If you can ssh to a machine, you can use scp.

docs.title: 2. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
scp [options] source destination

At least one side must be remote.

docs.title: 3. Copy a File from Local to Remote
docs.text:
scp file.txt user@remote:/home/user/

Example:

scp notes.txt mql@192.168.1.100:/home/mql/

docs.title: 4. Copy a File from Remote to Local
docs.text: scp user@remote:/home/user/file.txt .

docs.title: 5. Copy Directories (-r)
docs.text:
scp -r project/ user@remote:/home/user/

Copy from remote:

scp -r user@remote:/home/user/project/ .

docs.title: 6. Commonly Used Options (Beginner Must-Know)
docs.text: Option Meaning -r Copy directories recursively -P SSH port (uppercase) -v Verbose output -C Enable compression -p Preserve timestamps and mode -i Specify SSH key

docs.title: 7. Specify SSH Port (-P)
docs.text:
scp -P 2222 file.txt user@remote:/home/user/

⚠️ -P is uppercase.

docs.title: 8. Use SSH Key Authentication (-i)
docs.text: scp -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 file.txt user@remote:/home/user/

docs.title: 9. Copy Multiple Files
docs.text: scp a.txt b.txt user@remote:/home/user/

docs.title: 10. Show Progress and Details
docs.text: scp -v bigfile.iso user@remote:/data/

docs.title: 11. Preserve File Attributes (-p)
docs.text:
scp -p config.conf user@remote:/etc/

Preserves:

  • Modification time
  • Permissions

docs.title: 12. Use Compression (-C)
docs.text:
scp -C bigfile user@remote:/backup/

Useful for slow networks.

docs.title: Upload a File to a Server
docs.text: scp app.tar.gz user@server:/opt/apps/

docs.title: Download Log Files
docs.text: scp user@server:/var/log/messages .

docs.title: Copy Between Two Remote Servers
docs.text:
scp user1@host1:/data/file user2@host2:/backup/

(Your machine acts as a relay.)

docs.title: ❌ Forgetting -r for Directories
docs.text: scp folder user@remote:/tmp

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: scp -r folder user@remote:/tmp

docs.title: ❌ Confusing -p and -P
docs.text:
scp -p 2222 file.txt ...

❌ Wrong.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: scp -P 2222 file.txt ...

docs.title: ❌ Wrong Path
docs.text: scp file.txt user@remote:home/user

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text: scp file.txt user@remote:/home/user/

docs.title: 15. Security Tips
docs.text:
• Always verify the SSH host fingerprint
  • Use SSH keys instead of passwords
  • Avoid copying files as root unless necessary

docs.title: 16. scp vs rsync
docs.text:
Feature scp rsync Simple ✅ ❌ Resume transfer ❌ ✅ Sync directories ❌ ✅ Encrypted ✅ ✅

📌 Use scp for simple copy, rsync for sync/backup.

docs.title: 17. Quick Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
scp file user@host:/path
scp user@host:/path/file .
scp -r dir user@host:/path
scp -P 22 file user@host:/path
scp -i key file user@host:/path

docs.title: 18. Summary
docs.text:
• scp copies files securely over SSH
  • Easy to use for quick transfers
  • Supports files and directories
  • Depends on SSH configuration

docs.title: 1. What is wget?
docs.text:
wget is a command-line tool used to download files from the internet. It supports HTTP, HTTPS, and FTP protocols and works well even on unstable networks.

Typical use cases:

  • Download software packages
  • Fetch files from a server
  • Download files in the background
  • Mirror a website

docs.title: 2. Check if wget is installed
docs.text:
Most Linux distributions come with wget preinstalled.

wget --version

If not installed:

docs.title: Ubuntu / Debian
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install wget

docs.title: CentOS / Rocky / AlmaLinux
docs.text: sudo yum install wget

docs.title: Download a file
docs.text:
wget https://example.com/file.zip

The file will be saved in the current directory.

docs.title: Specify output filename
docs.text: wget -O myfile.zip https://example.com/file.zip

docs.title: Download to a specific directory
docs.text: wget -P /tmp https://example.com/file.zip

docs.title: 4. Download in the background
docs.text:
Useful for large files or remote servers.

wget -b https://example.com/large.iso

Check progress:

tail -f wget-log

docs.title: 5. Resume an interrupted download
docs.text:
If a download stops due to network issues:

wget -c https://example.com/large.iso

(-c means continue)

docs.title: From a list file
docs.text:
Create a file:

vim urls.txt

Example content:

https://example.com/a.zip
https://example.com/b.zip
https://example.com/c.zip

Download all:

wget -i urls.txt

docs.title: 7. Limit download speed
docs.text:
Useful to avoid consuming all bandwidth.

wget --limit-rate=500k https://example.com/file.iso

docs.title: HTTP username & password
docs.text:
wget --user=username --password=password https://example.com/file

⚠️ Passwords may appear in shell history.

docs.title: 9. Download an entire website (basic)
docs.text:
wget -r https://example.com

Common options:

  • -r → recursive
  • -np → no parent directory
  • -k → convert links for local use
  • -p → download page assets (CSS, images)

Example:

wget -r -np -k -p https://example.com

docs.title: 10. Common options summary
docs.text: Option Meaning -O Output filename -P Output directory -c Resume download -b Background download -i Read URLs from file -r Recursive download --limit-rate Limit speed

docs.title: 11. When to use wget vs curl
docs.text:
wget curl Best for downloading files Best for APIs & requests Supports recursive downloads No recursion Simple and robust More flexible

👉 Rule of thumb: Use wget for downloading files, curl for interacting with APIs.

docs.title: 12. Practice examples
docs.text:
wget https://example.com/test.txt
wget -c https://example.com/large.iso
wget -P ~/Downloads https://example.com/app.tar.gz

docs.title: 13. Get help
docs.text:
wget --help
man wget

docs.title: ✅ Summary
docs.text:
• wget is a powerful and simple download tool
  • Ideal for servers, scripts, and automation
  • Works well over slow or unstable networks

docs.title: 1. What is XDG?
docs.text:
XDG stands for X Desktop Group (now under freedesktop.org).

In simple terms:

XDG defines a set of standards that help Linux applications know where to store files and how to integrate with the desktop environment.

Before XDG, every application put files wherever it wanted, which led to chaos in $HOME. XDG brings order.

docs.title: 2. Why XDG Exists
docs.text:
Without XDG:

  • Config files scattered everywhere (~/.app1, ~/.config2, ~/random)
  • Cache files mixed with important data
  • Desktop menus inconsistent across environments

With XDG:

  • Predictable directories
  • Cleaner home directory
  • Desktop-agnostic behavior (works on GNOME, KDE, XFCE, etc.)

docs.title: 3. The Core Idea: XDG Base Directory Specification
docs.text: The XDG Base Directory Specification defines standard environment variables that applications should follow.

docs.title: The Most Important XDG Variables
docs.text:
Variable Purpose Default Location XDG_CONFIG_HOME User configuration files ~/.config XDG_DATA_HOME User data files ~/.local/share XDG_CACHE_HOME Cache files ~/.cache XDG_STATE_HOME State files (logs, history) ~/.local/state

If these variables are not set, applications must fall back to the defaults above.

docs.title: 4.1 Configuration → XDG_CONFIG_HOME
docs.text:
• Settings you want to keep
  • Human-readable
  • Safe to back up

Example:

~/.config/
 ├── git/config
 ├── nvim/init.lua
 └── alacritty/alacritty.yml

docs.title: 4.2 Data → XDG_DATA_HOME
docs.text:
• Persistent user data
  • Databases, icons, plugins

Example:

~/.local/share/
 ├── applications/
 ├── fonts/
 └── nvim/site/

docs.title: 4.3 Cache → XDG_CACHE_HOME
docs.text:
• Temporary data
  • Safe to delete

Example:

~/.cache/
 ├── pip/
 ├── thumbnails/
 └── chromium/

docs.title: 4.4 State → XDG_STATE_HOME
docs.text:
• Logs, history, runtime state
  • Not exactly config, not exactly cache

Example:

~/.local/state/
 ├── bash/history
 └── nvim/log

docs.title: 5. Viewing Your XDG Settings
docs.text:
Check current values:

echo $XDG_CONFIG_HOME
echo $XDG_DATA_HOME
echo $XDG_CACHE_HOME

If nothing prints, defaults are being used.

docs.title: 6. Setting XDG Variables (Optional)
docs.text:
You can customize XDG directories in your shell config:

# ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc
export XDG_CONFIG_HOME="$HOME/.config"
export XDG_DATA_HOME="$HOME/.local/share"
export XDG_CACHE_HOME="$HOME/.cache"
export XDG_STATE_HOME="$HOME/.local/state"

💡 Most users keep defaults — changing them is optional.

docs.title: Good Citizens (Follow XDG)
docs.text:
• git
  • neovim
  • systemd
  • pip
  • flatpak

docs.title: Bad / Legacy Apps
docs.text:
• Still write to ~/.appname
  • Ignore XDG variables

You’ll often see people migrate old dotfiles into ~/.config.

docs.title: 8. XDG Desktop Integration (Quick Intro)
docs.text: Beyond directories, XDG also defines:

docs.title: .desktop files
docs.text:
Used for application launchers.

Location:

~/.local/share/applications/

Example:

[Desktop Entry]
Name=My App
Exec=my-app
Type=Application

docs.title: MIME types & default apps
docs.text:
Managed via:

xdg-mime
xdg-open

Example:

xdg-open file.pdf

This opens the file using your system’s default app.

docs.title: 9. Common XDG Tools You’ll See
docs.text:
Command Purpose xdg-open Open file/URL with default app xdg-mime Query/set default applications xdg-user-dirs Manage Documents, Downloads, etc.

Example:

xdg-user-dir DOCUMENTS

docs.title: 10. Why XDG Matters to You
docs.text:
If you:

  • Manage dotfiles
  • Write Linux apps
  • Use multiple desktop environments
  • Care about clean $HOME

Then understanding XDG is essential.

docs.title: 11. One-Sentence Summary
docs.text: XDG is a Linux standard that tells applications where to store config, data, cache, and how to integrate with the desktop in a clean, predictable way.

docs.title: 1. What Is OpenWrt?
docs.text:
OpenWrt is an open-source embedded Linux operating system designed mainly for routers and network devices.

Unlike stock router firmware (from TP-Link, Xiaomi, ASUS, etc.), OpenWrt is:

  • A full Linux system
  • Highly customizable
  • Regularly updated
  • Designed for advanced networking

You can think of OpenWrt as:

“Ubuntu for routers”

docs.title: 2.1 Limitations of Stock Router Firmware
docs.text:
Most factory firmware:

  • Has limited features
  • Cannot install extra software
  • Is rarely updated
  • Locks advanced configuration

docs.title: 2.2 Advantages of OpenWrt
docs.text:
OpenWrt gives you:

  • ✅ Full control over routing, firewall, Wi-Fi
  • ✅ Package manager (opkg)
  • ✅ SSH access
  • ✅ Advanced features:
    • VLAN
    • VPN (WireGuard / OpenVPN)
    • QoS / SQM
    • Ad-blocking
    • Mesh networking
  • ✅ Long-term community support

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text: Component Purpose LuCI Web management interface opkg Package manager BusyBox Core Linux utilities netifd Network management procd Init system

docs.title: 4. Supported Devices
docs.text:
OpenWrt runs on:

  • Home routers
  • Travel routers
  • Mini PCs
  • Virtual machines
  • Raspberry Pi

docs.title: Check Compatibility
docs.text:
Always check:

https://openwrt.org/toh/start

Look for:

  • Flash size
  • RAM size
  • Supported version
  • Installation method

docs.title: 5.1 Common Installation Methods
docs.text:
Method When Used Web upgrade Stock firmware supports it TFTP Recovery mode Serial console Advanced / bricked devices SD card Raspberry Pi

⚠️ Installing the wrong firmware can brick your router.

docs.title: 6. First Boot Experience
docs.text: After installation:

docs.title: 6.1 Default Access
docs.text:
• IP address: 192.168.1.1
  • Username: root
  • Password: (empty)

docs.title: 6.2 First Things to Do
docs.text:
1. Set root password
  2. Configure WAN
  3. Enable Wi-Fi
  4. Update package list

docs.title: 7. LuCI Web Interface
docs.text: LuCI is OpenWrt’s web UI.

docs.title: Key Sections
docs.text:
Menu Description Status System & network status Network Interfaces, Wi-Fi, DHCP System Time, users, startup Services Installed services Firewall Zones, rules, NAT

LuCI makes OpenWrt usable without command-line knowledge.

docs.title: 8.1 Connect via SSH
docs.text: ssh root@192.168.1.1

docs.title: 8.2 Useful Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose opkg update Update package list opkg install xxx Install package opkg list-installed List packages reboot Reboot system logread View system logs

docs.title: 9. Package Management with opkg
docs.text: OpenWrt uses opkg, similar to apt or yum.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
opkg update
opkg install htop

Common packages:

  • luci-app-wireguard
  • luci-app-openvpn
  • luci-app-adblock
  • luci-app-sqm

docs.title: Interfaces
docs.text:
OpenWrt uses logical interfaces:

  • LAN
  • WAN
  • WAN6

Each interface includes:

  • Protocol (DHCP, PPPoE, Static)
  • Firewall zone
  • Physical device

docs.title: Wi-Fi
docs.text:
Wi-Fi is disabled by default after installation. You must:

  1. Set SSID
  2. Set encryption
  3. Enable radio

docs.title: 11. Firewall Concept (Very Important)
docs.text: OpenWrt firewall is zone-based.

docs.title: Common Zones
docs.text:
Zone Purpose LAN Trusted internal network WAN Untrusted internet

Default rule:

LAN → WAN : allowed
WAN → LAN : blocked

This provides strong security by default.

docs.title: 12. Common Use Cases
docs.text:
OpenWrt is often used for:

  • Home router replacement
  • VPN gateway
  • Ad-blocking router
  • Smart home network isolation
  • Campus / dorm networking
  • Learning networking & Linux

docs.title: 13. When OpenWrt Is NOT Recommended
docs.text:
Avoid OpenWrt if:

  • Your router has very small flash (<8MB)
  • You only want “plug and play”
  • You are afraid of troubleshooting

docs.title: 14. Learning Path Recommendation
docs.text:
For beginners:

  1. Learn LuCI basics
  2. Understand WAN / LAN
  3. Learn opkg
  4. Try SSH
  5. Configure VPN
  6. Learn firewall rules

docs.title: 15. Summary
docs.text:
OpenWrt is:

  • A powerful Linux-based router OS
  • Highly flexible and customizable
  • Ideal for learning networking
  • Best for users who want control

If you know basic Linux, OpenWrt will feel very natural.

docs.title: 1. What Is VNC?
docs.text:
VNC (Virtual Network Computing) lets you control your Raspberry Pi’s desktop remotely from another computer (Windows, macOS, or Linux) over the network.

Raspberry Pi OS includes RealVNC Server by default, so no extra installation is usually needed.

docs.title: 2. Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, make sure:

  • Raspberry Pi is running Raspberry Pi OS (with desktop)
  • Raspberry Pi is connected to the network (Wi-Fi or Ethernet)
  • You can:
    • Access the Pi locally (monitor + keyboard), or
    • Log in via SSH

docs.title: 3. Enable VNC on Raspberry Pi OS (GUI Method)
Step 1: Open Raspberry Pi Configuration
docs.text:
1. Click the Raspberry Pi menu (top-left)
  2. Select Preferences
  3. Click Raspberry Pi Configuration

docs.title: Step 2: Enable VNC
docs.text:
1. Switch to the Interfaces tab
  2. Find VNC
  3. Select Enable
  4. Click OK

✅ VNC Server is now running.

docs.title: 4. Enable VNC via Command Line (SSH / Terminal Method)
docs.text: If you don’t have a monitor attached, use this method.

docs.title: Step 1: Open Terminal or SSH into Pi
docs.text:
ssh pi@<raspberry-pi-ip>

(Default user may be pi or your custom username.)

docs.title: Step 2: Run Raspberry Pi Configuration Tool
docs.text: sudo raspi-config

docs.title: Step 3: Enable VNC
docs.text:
1. Select Interface Options
  2. Select VNC
  3. Choose Yes
  4. Select OK
  5. Exit

docs.title: 5. Verify VNC Service Status
docs.text:
Check whether the VNC server is running:

systemctl status vncserver-x11-serviced

You should see:

Active: active (running)

If not running, start it manually:

sudo systemctl enable vncserver-x11-serviced
sudo systemctl start vncserver-x11-serviced

docs.title: 6. Find Raspberry Pi IP Address
docs.text: You need the Pi’s IP address to connect.

docs.title: Method 1: From Raspberry Pi
docs.text:
hostname -I

Example output:

192.168.1.120

docs.title: Method 2: From Router
docs.text:
• Log into your router admin page
  • Find DHCP / Connected Devices
  • Look for raspberrypi

docs.title: 7.1 Install VNC Viewer
docs.text:
Download RealVNC Viewer on your client computer:

  • https://www.realvnc.com/en/connect/download/viewer/

Available for:

  • Windows
  • macOS
  • Linux

docs.title: 7.2 Connect to Raspberry Pi
docs.text:
1. Open VNC Viewer

  2. Enter the Pi’s IP address:

    192.168.1.120

  3. Press Enter

  4. Click Continue (security warning is normal)

  5. Enter:

    • Username: Pi username
    • Password: Pi password

🎉 You should now see the Raspberry Pi desktop.

docs.title: Problem 1: Black Screen After Connecting
docs.text:
Cause: No active desktop session.

Fix:

  • Ensure Raspberry Pi OS with desktop is installed

  • Reboot the Pi:

    sudo reboot

docs.title: Problem 2: Connection Refused
docs.text:
Check:

systemctl status vncserver-x11-serviced

If stopped:

sudo systemctl start vncserver-x11-serviced

docs.title: Problem 3: Slow Performance
docs.text:
Suggestions:

  • Use Ethernet instead of Wi-Fi
  • Lower resolution:
    • Raspberry Pi Configuration → Display → Resolution
  • Close unnecessary programs

docs.title: 9. (Optional) Start VNC Automatically on Boot
docs.text:
Usually enabled by default, but you can confirm:

sudo systemctl enable vncserver-x11-serviced

docs.title: 10. Security Tips (Important for Beginners)
docs.text:
• Change default password

    passwd

  • Do NOT expose VNC directly to the internet

  • Use VNC only in local network, or via SSH tunnel

docs.title: 11. Summary
docs.text:
